questions.scenario: The Orders API generates PDF invoices that customers download later. The files are immutable after creation, support needs to retrieve them by order context, and the application instances will scale in and out during the day. Which Azure storage shape is the best first fit for the invoice files themselves?

options.text: Azure Blob Storage, because the application is storing durable file-like objects rather than relational rows or instance-local state.
Azure SQL Database, because storing the PDF bytes in relational rows makes later downloads easier to scale.
The local filesystem on the web app instances, because the files are generated by the same application that serves them.
Azure Files, because a mounted shared filesystem is the default first choice for immutable customer download objects.

questions.explanation: Blob Storage is the right first fit because the workload is durable object storage for generated files. The closest distractor is Azure Files, because it is also a valid Azure storage service for shared file access, but this scenario is about object-shaped downloads rather than a mounted filesystem contract. Azure SQL Database is not the right primary home for object-shaped data, and instance-local disks disappear with compute changes. See [Introduction to Azure Blob Storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blobs-introduction) and [Introduction to Azure Files](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/files/storage-files-introduction).

questions.scenario: A team is moving receipt files from local disks into Azure Blob Storage. The same design review has to answer three questions before migration is approved: where the security and management boundary should live, how support will reach files without public access, and how accidental deletion will be recovered. Which two design areas must be settled first because they define the core storage boundary and operational path?

options.text: The storage account and container boundaries that will hold the objects.
The blob naming and metadata pattern used for lookup.
The access path, identity model, and recovery controls for those objects.
The lifecycle and tiering policy that will move old blobs between hot, cool, or archive storage.

questions.explanation: The storage account and container establish the object boundary, while the access path, identity model, and recovery controls decide whether those objects are private, reachable, and recoverable in production. The closest distractor is naming and metadata, because those matter for lookup and should be designed well, but they do not replace choosing the account and access boundary first. Lifecycle and tiering rules are important later, but they do not define the core private-access storage boundary on their own. See [Introduction to Azure Blob Storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blobs-introduction) and [Authorize access to blobs with Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/authorize-access-azure-active-directory).

questions.scenario: An internal admin app writes uploaded CSV exports to a local directory on each App Service instance. During a scale-out event, users report that files uploaded minutes earlier are missing from half the requests, and a redeployment removes the rest. What is the most accurate review finding?

options.text: The local path is acceptable if the team scales up the App Service plan so each instance has more disk capacity for uploads.
The app should switch the upload path to the App Service content share, because any shared platform filesystem is equivalent to object storage for this workload.
User uploads need durable shared object storage such as Blob Storage instead of relying on instance-local filesystem state.
The fix is to keep writing to local disk but add more aggressive backup jobs so scale events do not remove recently uploaded files.

questions.explanation: The root problem is assuming local application storage behaves like durable shared object storage. The closest distractor is switching to a shared platform filesystem, because that can sound like enough centralization, but the article's decision boundary is object storage for uploaded files that must survive compute churn and support reliable retrieval. More local disk and backup jobs also miss that primary durability boundary. See [Introduction to Azure Blob Storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blobs-introduction).

questions.scenario: A developer proposes one public container for customer avatars, downloadable invoices, and internal finance exports because all three are file uploads. The application can generate signed download paths when needed, but the developer argues that one public container is simpler to manage. What should the reviewer challenge first?

options.text: The container layout, because public avatars and private document classes should at least be separated into different containers even before finer access rules are discussed.
Mixing objects with very different sensitivity into one broad public access boundary without deliberate separation and access controls.
The SAS issuance model, because signed URLs can constrain who downloads invoices even if the container itself remains public.
The lifecycle policy, because the main danger is moving internal exports to a cooler tier before support has downloaded them.

questions.explanation: The first review challenge is the access boundary itself: public avatars, private invoices, and internal exports should not automatically share one anonymous container. The closest distractor is the SAS idea, because signed download paths are useful for controlled access, but they do not undo the broader mistake of making the whole container public. Lifecycle and naming decisions matter later, but the main problem is exposure. See [Configure anonymous read access for containers and blobs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/anonymous-read-access-configure) and [Grant limited access to Azure Storage resources using shared access signatures](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview).

questions.scenario: The finance team wants a lifecycle rule that archives invoice PDFs after 30 days and deletes them after 90 days. Legal then clarifies that invoices must remain recoverable for 180 days. What is the strongest storage review finding?

options.text: The policy is acceptable because archive tier keeps the blob content recoverable even after the lifecycle delete action runs.
The team should keep the 90-day delete rule but enable blob versioning, because versioning overrides the need for a longer retention window.
The team should keep the archive step and move the delete action to 180 days, because matching the delete timer to the legal retention statement is enough on its own.
The lifecycle policy conflicts with the stated recovery requirement and must be redesigned before it is enabled.

questions.explanation: A deletion rule at 90 days does not satisfy a 180-day recovery requirement. The closest distractor is moving the delete action to 180 days, because that is a reasonable part of the repair, but the review still has to check whether the full lifecycle and recovery design actually preserves the required recoverability window instead of only matching one timer to the policy text. Archive and cool tiers are valid storage decisions, but tiering does not override a delete action, and versioning still has to be designed around the required retention window. See [Lifecycle management for Azure Blob Storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/lifecycle-management-overview) and [Blob versioning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/versioning-overview).
